title: Welcome to The Luminous Library
author: Matt
body:
Welcome to The Luminous Library

This is an archive. A living, growing record of thoughts, code, papers, and projects.
A portfolio of a mind that couldn’t stop asking questions, and couldn’t stop building answers.

The Luminous Library exists for one reason, to contribute to humanity’s progress. Not in a grand or self-important way, but in the quiet, persistent way of
someone who believes knowledge should be free, accessible, and useful. For humanity to do better. Have better. Be better.

Think of this as a modern Library of Alexandria, focused on the domains shaping our next century:
technology, psychology, philosophy, physics, AI, cognitive science, systems theory… and whatever else exists between them.

If you’re reading this and something here resonates, if it feels like someone else sees what you see,

That’s why this exists.

Even if it reaches one person.

Even if it shifts one mind by the smallest possible degree.

That’s enough.

That will always be enough.

 Matt M., Luminal Systems

title: The Myers Fractal & MIP
author: Matt
body:
Hello everyone and thank you for coming back to the TLL blog, The Luminal Ledger.

Today, I'd like to share something I happened to stumble into by happy accident. Allow me to reveal a brand new fractal class, or set of fractals, that I've since named "The Myers Fractal" and the "Myers Interlock Principle." At its core, the Myers Fractal consists of two separate base fractals, Tree and H-Tree, replicated and propagated to all four cardinal directions simultaneously. At each new step in depth, both fractals are pushed outward, creating a synchronized expansion.

The Myers Interlock Principle is a governing methodology I’ve developed for getting two or more distinct fractals to coexist. This principle ensures new topological emergence within the structure without destructive interference; essentially, the new fractal retains its "fractal-ness." You can see the Myers Fractal in action right now on our homepage, where it rotates slowly, and in the Fractal Playground area, where it is shown by default.

The discovery was serendipitous. While attempting to get a Tree fractal to replicate in all directions, I took a break to walk my dog, and whilst doing so, stared endlessly at the trees in my backyard. I noticed how remarkably similar they looked to the fractal models I was building. Realizing that the primary difference between a standard Tree and an H-Tree was the angle offset, I wondered: what happens if I draw both at once? 

I overlaid a Tree and an H-Tree at the same origin point. After tinkering, I successfully pushed them in all four directions by copying the original Tree function, adjusting the angle offset (45 degrees vs. 90 degrees), and setting them to the same (0,0) starting coordinates.

By depth 3 or 4, a genuine, unique shape started to emerge. I began testing how different fractals mesh, which led to an interesting insight, that being, only fractals that are structurally similar can coexist and reshape into new topological emergence. For example, combining a Dragon Curve with a Sierpinski Triangle resulted in a mess. Even combining a Sierpinski Triangle and a Sierpinski Carpet, despite their shared logic, resulted in destructive interference patterns. This led me to conclude that the specific angle offsets of the original bases are the key variables allowing for a non-destructive union.

Using this idea, I’ve been able to create five entirely new fractals. One of them, the "Cross X," is a conjunction of a Tree and H-Tree rotated into an X and replicated, creating an infinite "cross-X" effect. 

For the Mathematical Framework, I have defined the Myers set as follows:
(I have formatted the math into LaTeX format)

Let $F(x, d)$ be a recursive fractal function with parameters $x$ (position) and $d$ (depth).

For Tree:
$$F_T(x, d) = x + \sum(branches) [ F_T(branch\_angle, d - 1) ]$$

For H-Tree:
$$F_H(x, d) = x + \sum(h-structure) [ F_H(next\_coords, d - 1) ]$$

The Myers Set is defined as:
$$M(x, d) = F_T(x, d) \oplus F_H(x, d)$$

Where $\oplus$ denotes a non-destructive recursive union. The Myers Interlock Principle guarantees:
* Orthogonality of direction
* Recursive parity ($d_T = d_H$)
* Cardinal preservation: $\{+X, -X, +Y, -Y\}$ propagation

However, you should note that the symbol chosen was completely random and really I just needed something that was not already in use. Lol. 

You can find a working example here: [Fractal Playground](https://luminalsystems.github.io/playground.html)

A conceptual whitepaper is available in the papers section [here](https://luminalsystems.github.io/papers/myers-fractal/). 
Depth renders from 1–13 are available in the repository.

Matt M., Luminal Systems.

title: The Fractal Playground
author: Matt
body:
The Fractal Playground started with me just trying to draw my own fractal and mess with it. I did a quick search to see if anything like it already existed, there is one out there, but I didn't like how it functioned. So I drew a canvas, started drawing fractals to it, and built my own.

You land on the page and you're met with the Myers Fractal by default, with a floating control panel on the right. Using the sliders, you can adjust the depth of any fractal. Using the checkboxes, you can toggle which fractals are drawn to the canvas, and you can check multiple at once. So if you check the Sierpinski Carpet at depth 3, the Myers Fractal at depth 7, and the Koch Snowflake at depth 9, all three draw simultaneously. Maybe something emergent comes from that combination. Maybe not. That's why it's a playground.

The fractal library includes some well-known classics like the Koch Snowflake, Sierpinski Triangle, and Dragon Curve, and several I built myself using the Myers Interlock Principle. Honestly, most of those were happy accidents discovered while building this thing in the first place. Lol.

TL;DR: It's an HTML5 canvas page that draws fractals, lets you layer them, adjust their depth, and see what happens when you combine them. PAFO - Play Around and Find Out. :}

You can explore it here: [Fractal Playground](https://luminalsystems.github.io/playground.html) And the project here : [Fractal Playground Project](./projects/playground/index.html) Matt M., Luminal Systems

title: Less is More - The TLL Blog
author: Matt
body:
This one started out of necessity.

I wanted to build a portfolio site and document my work, but I don't have money for hosting or domain names. GitHub Pages is free, but it only serves static front-end code, no server, no database, no typical web stack.

So I looked around for blog systems that actually worked on GitHub Pages. Found maybe two or three. None of them did what I wanted, and honestly, if the aesthetics throw me off I'm probably not using your code regardless of how functional it is. Lol.

So I built my own.

The TLL Blog -- The Luminal Ledger -- runs entirely on HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, with JSON flat files acting as a pseudo-database.
No backend. No server. No hosting costs. Just static files served directly from the repo.

It also works bidirectionally. Write posts on your localhost, hit "Sim Git Push," drop in your GitHub token, and it pushes posts.json to the live repo automatically. Your local environment and the live site stay in sync without ever touching a server.

I built it as a portfolio piece, then figured, someone else probably needs this too. So it's open source, free to use, and the working demo is the very blog you're reading right now.

You can find the project files here: [TLL Blog Project](./projects/TLL/index.html) Matt M., Luminal Systems
